When I started to really work on this in earnest a couple of months ago and then really in earnest the last four or five days, and I started to look at everybody's stuff, it occurred to me that when we talk about using the printed word in any media to make ourselves people of influence and to exert influence on others, that often problems start with lack of clarity about what it is we're trying to do in the first place. And so for the most part probably everybody in the room here has this in common. They're under multiple deadline pressures for multiple things. Just about everybody is doing one, many of you are doing two, three, and four newsletters and you're doing daily or weekly or hourly emails and trying to write books and products and so forth. And in many of those cases you're in full speed without really stopping to give a lot of thought to what the heck are we trying to accomplish with this collection of printed words that we put on print. Now it's a lot clearer obviously when you're doing a sales letter because you're trying to sell whatever the hell the thing is that's in the sales letter and that's pretty much as far as that goes and in many cases that's as far as it needs to go. But when you start doing these other things that may not necessarily directly elicit or solicit an order, then there's multiple things that can be accomplished and in each case there needs to be some kind of priority. And so I think we start with why do we write before we deal with how we write. So I made you a little list on page six and, well it's my little note here, most people on their questionnaires listed the first item has their number one. So most people said I'm coming to learn how to write better to attract customers, clients, patients, followers, donors, whatever. And a few people had also how to attract media attention. But certainly the majority had as number one we want to attract more customers and attract more people to us. And that's fine but it's really simplistic and it won't necessarily I think get you where you want to go. And so the first thing I'd say about that is that you really want to give a lot of thought to who you want to attract, not just quantity of attraction. And we talked a little bit about this yesterday on a Go Plus call, if you guys were on a Go Plus call. And I actually talk about it a lot and I think marketers in general trade away long term customer value and long term business value by not being really focused with the bait they put out into the marketplace about who it attracts. And it's fairly easy to attract quantity but value in a person is a whole other issue. And who might be a very, very valuable customer for you might be of little or no value for me or vice versa, even if in fact we basically sell the same things. Because if we don't really deeply resonate with them, then they're valued us over a period of time, disintegrates, the retention isn't good and so forth. So one of the things that we want to look at is we spend these days together and we talk about attraction, writing to attract is not just attracting anybody and everybody and the greatest quantity that we can get. And in fact measuring success by like response percentages has opposed to measuring success by how many of the right people do we put together in one place at one time. Second then the reason that we want to write in the way that we're going to talk about is connection. And connection means that there's more to it than monetary transactions. It means that the person on the other end of all this communication has feelings about us, has a result of the communication and has a sense that we have feelings about them. And so that's what connection is about. Acceptance of advocated positions. So a number of people on the questionnaires and that's certainly a reason to try and write, be it print or speech or whatever. And we have sort of a real lab in front of us in the moment because we have of course political debates and a political campaign underway. And so a lot of what we talk about, there's great examples on TV every night of people getting it right, people getting it wrong, people screwing it up and trying to fix it, people trying to figure it out. But acceptance of advocated position is another reason to try and write influential. We're not just trying to get a customer, we're not just trying to sell a product or a series of products, we're trying to get buy-in to our positions to what we're all about. And that can be all the way to the most kind of micro practical of those positions like long copy is better than short copy or trying to convince somebody that if you're in the do it for them newsletter business that ugly is better than pretty. So they need to buy into that position all the way to the macro philosophical stuff that actually is more important in keeping customers. Sales and purchases of course we write to sell things. Involvement which is the hardest of all of these things is actually getting the people interactive with us rather than passive with us. And it's the toughest thing of all to do. If you look at newsletters as a microcosm and like you look at us, we are perennially running little contests and trying to get bills doing spot the fatal flaw and you win something and I'm giving away a car and the percentage of people who actually participate in any of that is painfully low. I'm convinced there's a lot of people who think they participated and actually never did it. But the percentage is painfully low and it tends to be of course the same people over and over and over again. Every time you get somebody new to do it though, you move them much deeper into commitment to you and into your culture which is why we keep working on these involvement things. And in many cases keep making them dumber and dumber and dumber and easier and easier and easier for people to do. It's like send us a piece of paper with a big black mark on it and you win. I'm assuming right that one of the reasons we fill out the four or five page question here before we came here was to position us. Yeah, well yeah and I'll tell you something about that. Not that I have any concern about it but I mean I do it sort of reflexively. So Charlie said to me back in the room before we started he said well you know I'm really looking forward to this and it's going to be good and it better be good. You know meaning you guys paid a lot of money to be here and so it better be good. And I said to him that really how good I am won't really matter much because it's a combination of the who, the buy-in to the who, the expectation, the price paid, the pre-event involvement. Now the expectation of it being good is so high. It's good whether it's good or not. And so involvement helps the person be satisfied with what they're getting. It also by the way in many cases delivers value regardless of...
